sentences.text: Yes, this would-- this would be-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]
All the ringers are off.
[LAUGHTER]
This was the-- I would say, the late fall of 1939 and probably the winter of 1940 that we were able to have classes.
Because then, in the spring of 1940, that was not possible anymore.
That has ended.
We had to work.
And it just-- people were being evacuated.
People were being sent away for resettling.
And--
Yes, every-- almost, almost everybody I knew.
I, I think that it was because my father had a job.
My father worked in the company for which he traveled, for which he was a salesman.
The company was owned by Jewish people.
And they escaped.
At the very beginning of the war, they went towards-- to Russia.
They went to Lwow, Lemberg.
And then they went to Russia.
And so one of the boys-- my-- the superintendent of the building-- one of her younger sons was working for the company.
My father gave him a job.
He became the Treuhander.
He became the trustee of the company.
And my father worked for him.
But actually, my father was, you know, running the business because he didn't know enough about it.
And I think because this made my father work for them that he had a job.
And we were able to stay still.
But a lot of people were told they had to leave.
And they did not get a permit.
We-- what was happening is that we had to get a permit to remain in Cracow.
And I think, first of all, anybody under 12 would not be allowed to remain in Cracow.
And I think anybody over 55 would not remain in Cracow.
You had to get a permit.
And my father was able to get a permit for my mother and myself.
I was 10 years old.
But they changed my birth certificate to make me 12.
And my father was able to obtain a permit for some of his brothers.
Some of his brothers were working.
Some-- a couple of his brothers were-- and his father were house painters.
So the Germans needed them.
My, my father-- my mother's brothers, who-- they could not get a permit.
And they had to leave.
And I think that they were sent to Tarnow, to another-- outside of Cracow.
And my mother's sister and her husband with the two little girls, my cousins Rita and Jenny, they could not remain.
They had to leave.
My grandparents were able to stay.
I am sure my father was able to arrange it.
And my mother's younger sister stayed.
In the building where I lived, a lot of my neighbors could not stay.
And they had to relocate.
They had to be relocated.
So-- and then of course, the Germans started the Aktions.
And they started to grab people off the street, people from work.
And people were being taken away every day.
I saw-- I saw it happening from far away a couple of times where I was on a street.
I had to work.
We all had to work.
And most of the time, my father was somehow able to arrange for me not to have to work on the streets, to clean the streets, to sweep the street.
And the few times that he couldn't, and I was sweeping the streets, cleaning the streets of manure, I saw the Germans rounding up the Hasidic Jews on the old part of town in Cracow, Kazimierz.
And they would round up the Hasidic Jews.
They would cut their hair.
They would shave them.
They would pull at their beard.
And then they would take them away-- the families, lots of families just were [SIGHS] destroyed.
Because not only they were taken-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they were taking parents away from the children, fathers away from the families.
So there were a lot of children.
Sometimes parents would hide the children.
And they never came back for them because they were caught on the streets looking for food.
We were very strictly rationed.
And for what you could buy, you had to stand in line for the bread.
I remember when we lived right around the corner from the bakery.
And at 3 o'clock in the morning, I would go and stand in line to buy-- to buy bread.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had to stand in line while we were still in Cracow to buy coal in a bucket, you know.
You had-- we were very strict rations.
And of course, since we were not paid, people were not working.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
They had no money.
When I didn't have to-- I worked in a-- I already work-- actually, if I didn't keep-- have to go on the street, I think my father kept me home.
I was still able to, to be home.
This was just the time while they were grabbing people, taking people away, and already getting the ghetto ready-- already building the ghetto.
My mother also was able to avoid the hard labor in the beginning because of my father.
But also, a couple of times, we had to go and help with building the wall around the ghetto.
This was in 1940, probably in the summer or in the fall.
And then, of course, the time came we had to go into the ghetto.
My house was outside, yes.
The ghetto was in Podgorze, which was the other side of the river.
And we were told-- of course, we couldn't own radios.
But they had, like, flyers hanging on every corner of the street that the order was for the Jewish people to-- they would have to leave their homes by such and such date.
And you have to be in the ghetto by March 6th, I believe, 1941.
And we're not allowed to take anything.
Remember, a lot of time was standing-- spent standing in line to get a, a room assigned in the ghetto.
Because you had to find a place to, to live.
And of course, we couldn't get an apartment.
You know, we knew we wouldn't be getting an apartment.
We would be getting a, a room that we would have to share with other families.
And that was--
Because we were told.
We were told.
And that was-- I mean, that was very scary.
Here I have always lived in this apartment that-- you know, my home.
And I had nice things.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And here we had to leave everything behind.
You couldn't take enough things.
We could only take, like, one bed and only a small suitcase each person.
And also, my father, again, was able to arrange to get a pushcart.
So we could put things in, like pots and pans and bedding and the bed.
I remember the bed.
And we just had to walk out from the house.
Everything was left there because we couldn't sell anything, because the Poles knew that we couldn't take anything.
So I, I-- so they didn't-- nobody would buy it.
I remember my mother packed a trunk with some linens and stuff-- I don't know what she put it in it-- and gave it to the superintendent of the building to save for us.
But a lot of-- probably she gave her-- a lot-- everything probably that she couldn't take.
She gave her some clothing.
And, and we just left.
We just left, walked through the streets of the ghetto, through Cracow.
And we, you know, we couldn't walk on the sidewalk.
We walked on the street.
And the streets are very muddy, you know.
They're not like the streets-- United States.
It was very muddy.
And there was a lot of, you know, horses.
So there's manure.
And we just walked.
And the Polish people stood on the sidewalk.
And they were yelling, says, good riddance.
Go!
Don't come back!
Such hate-- there was such hate, such unbelievable hate.
And then when we got into the ghetto, one of my father's brother had an apartment in the ghetto, a very small apartment.
It had two rooms and a kitchen.
It was an old building.
Most of the-- most of the places in the ghetto were, you know, old, old places, old building.
So we moved in with my uncle and his wife and a child.
And we were in the-- in the one small room.
And they had another room.
And there was a small kitchen.
And then we had to share our room with another couple and, and a sister of the man whose name was Marcel Goldberg.
Marcel Goldberg with his wife, Lusza, and his sister, Sitka, lived with us in the ghetto.
So that this tiny room that we had-- we had, like, a-- was divided by a string with a-- with a blanket hanging, so divide those two-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] those, those, those two families together.
So I think they had one bed.
And my mother and father had one bed.
And I slept on a cot.
And Marcel's sister, Sitka, slept on a cot.
Six people in that small room-- really tiny narrow room.
And we, we felt lucky because we knew everybody in the apartment.
And-- Did you know Marcel Goldberg before, before the--
I said, I never knew him before.
But I-- obviously my father did, and my mother did.
So--
Well, in the ghetto, we-- in the ghetto, we all had to work.
If you didn't work, you couldn't stay.

So in the ghetto-- again, because my father knew so many people-- he arranged that I had a job in a-- what they called 'Papier Gemeinschaft.' It was a-- it was a place where they were making-- working with papers.
They, they got-- they were-- they got bales of plain paper.
And they would process it through machines, make it into notebooks.
And I work on one of the machines.
And we had to-- and so did my mother.
And it was, like, from 6 o'clock at-- in the morning to 6 o'clock at night, one shift.
And then the night shift was, like, from 6 o'clock at night to 6 o'clock in the morning.
But I really never had to work a whole night.
I was on the night shift.
Somehow, my father was always able to-- just brought me home.
By 10 o'clock at night, I would be home.
I, I--
My mother, too-- my mother, too.
Actually, I think that my mother and I would-- we'd never work on the same shift in the beginning together, for whatever reason.
I don't know.
But my father had an awful lot of friends.
And everybody liked him.
And he just could do anything.

So that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in, in the family that, that came to ghetto, like my father's family, brothers, and sisters-in-law, he was a-- and my grandparents, my mother's parents, and my mother younger sister, he was able to help us-- help a lot when they were being-- when every few months, you had to go and get another permit to remain.
And my father was able to get permits for, for other people.
Then one of my uncle became-- my father and one of my uncles became members of the Ordnungsdienst, the Jewish police.
And Marcel Goldberg, my father arranged for him to become a police.
So I think they had clout.
They could arrange things.
They knew a little ahead of time when the Germans were coming, when there would be, you know, when, when there be another selection.
And they would protect people.
So for, for a few months, I think that they were able to help themselves and a lot of people.
One, one time, one of my girlfriend's mother was being taken away.
And they came right-- came to my father.
And he went to help her and get her back, you know.
But a few months later, she went.
They took her.
And the same thing happened with my grandparents, my mother's parents, that first few selections, my father was able to save them.
And then he couldn't do anything.
At one selection, my father didn't get a, a permit.
And he and my mother were getting ready to, to leave to-- with a transport.
And then, at the last minute, one of the Germans that he knew arranged for him to get a permit.
They were talking about leaving.
And I was going to stay with my-- some other aunt and uncle and my cousins in the ghetto.
It was-- I mean, every, every second of your life, your life was in danger.
And you didn't know when you went to work in the morning if you will come back at night.
Or if you were, were home at night, if somebody would not knock at your door, and the Gestapo or the soldiers would come in and take you.
You never knew.
My--
I had-- we had no idea.
I had no idea.
I don't think anybody knew.

We all-- we were told in the beginning that all those people are going to be sent to the farm, that they will help work in, in-- on farms and work in the fields to help grow  food for the army-- for the German Army.
And people believed it.
And then I remember some people who were taken away.
And then a few weeks later, they would get a, a telegram that their loved one died.
And also, I remember in the beginning, people-- some people would actually get ashes.
They say, these are the remains of your father, or brother, or mother, or sister.
At the very beginning-- after that, that stopped.
After a while, it stopped.
They had no idea.
They had no idea.
[SIGHS] I think the rumors were slowly started in 1940.
But I also think it took us a while before anybody would believe it.
And there was such-- you know, there was so much killing in the ghetto.
I mean, my father had a very good friend.
His name was Rudy.
And my father used to play cards with him all the time.
And one day, my father came running home.
His friend Rudy was shot in front of the hospital.
I think he was working there.
There was no reason.
There was no reason.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was never a reason to, to kill anybody-- just because we were in the ghetto.
My mother's parents [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was the last selection.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And again, I don't know anything for sure.
Because my mother and I-- we never talked about it after the war.
And then when I was ready to ask questions, my mother was already gone.
So there was nobody for me to ask questions.
But one day during a selection, I was told to take-- to go downstairs with my grandparents.
Now, we didn't live in the same building.
My grandparents came to the building where we lived.
And we were told to hide.
My mother told me-- my mother and her younger sister-- we lived in an old building.
And it was like a block, because the entrance to the front was on the side that was already outside of the ghetto.
So we had-- so of course, it was locked-- boarded, couldn't use it.
The, the back of it was connected with the street behind.
And it had a big courtyard, huge courtyard.
It was all-- there was big bushes and big trees.
And my grandfather and my grandmother and myself, we laid down under the bushes.
My mother and her sister covered us with leaves.
And they left.
And they told us to lay quietly.
And we did.
All day long, I could see the patrol coming to the courtyard.
They were looking around.
They went into the building.
Nobody was supposed to be in the building.
It was empty.
People either went to work, or people were taken away and put on a [NON-ENGLISH], which was an old marketplace.
They kept putting people there.
And you could hear, you know, the trucks.
You could hear shooting.
You could hear yelling and screaming.
And dogs were barking.
And people were crying.
Finally, it was getting quiet.
I remember it was very, very quiet.
And we just laid there.
And we laid there.
And my mother came in again with her sister.
And she says, it's all right.
You can come out now.
Everybody's gone.
So we got out.
We-- and we were standing there with my mother and my sister and getting cleaned off.
And all of a sudden, from the back, two soldiers came.
It was probably the last patrol of the day.
They were not supposed to be there anymore.
They were supposed to have left.
And, of course, they asked for papers.
And my grandparents didn't have any.
And they took them.
They took them.
And they went, I remember-- I just remember standing there with my mother and her sister.
And my grandmother and my grandfather were not that old.
They were probably 75 years old.
And they were walking away, you know, with nothing.
They were walking away.
And I remember also my mother.
She was just-- it was like she, she was fuming in her mouth, you know.
It was-- she-- it was-- it was horrible.
Of course, now I know that they probably went to Belzec.
The transport went to Belzec.
So they were-- by next day, they were not alive anymore.
But when they took them, we still thought that they would take them to work someplace.
They were still strong, well, not that strong.
It's not normal work they could do.
I think that I had hopes until I got to Auschwitz.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, you could hope that people could work and survive on a farm or work, work camps.
But I think after a while-- then the rumors-- when you realize the rumors were not rumors anymore, I don't think that we hoped anymore.
And, and then, of course, my father was arrested.
My father was arrested on the-- December 31, 1942.
And he was arrested by the Jewish police.
They came with a list.
Yes.
His name was given to them.
The Gestapo called and told them to arrest my father and Marcel Goldberg because somebody said that they were active in the Underground.
There was a resistance in the camp, in the ghetto, the young people.
They were-- they were very active.
I don't think my father had anything to do with them.
But--
I don't think so.
But I think that who reported them was a, a German Jew who was working for the Gestapo.
And I think that he was envious of my father, who had so many friends and who was so popular.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my father was held in the Jewish-- in the jail behind the Ordnungsdienst police for a week while we tried-- for a week or two weeks while we tried to bribe somebody to, to, to release him.
And Marcel Goldberg was released.
And my father was not.
And in January-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know if it was a week or two weeks-- in January, my father was taken on a transport.
And we knew he was going to go to Auschwitz.
And I went to see--
They told us.
They told us.
I only knew at that time it was just-- that it was a camp.
I knew nothing else.
And I went.
And I said goodbye to my father.
And they took my father away.
And I, I couldn't imagine that-- what would happen to us, my mother and me, without my father or-- and I always felt that my father was so smart.
And he was so strong.
Nothing could happen to him.
He would-- he would certainly be able to manage.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then I think, too, somebody told us.
And I, I know it was one of my cousins who came and told us that my father escaped.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And whether he did that because he wanted to give us hope, or he wanted our grandparents-- my father's parents were so, you know, devastated that this could have happened to my father.
And I remember that I was-- we packed some socks.
I was darning socks and hankies and some shirts and some clothes, and actually made a small package and gave it to my cousin.
My cousin was killed in Plaszow.
Again, there is nobody that I could ask.
Yes, they were still alive in the ghetto.
And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and my father also, just before he was arrested, he was able to save my little cousin from Berlin, my youngest cousin from Berlin, my mother's niece.
Somehow, I don't know how, he was able to.
He knew that they were being taken away from Tarnow-- her parents and the older sister.
And he brought this little girl to stay with us.
She was five years old.
So when my father left-- my father was taken away, my mother and I, we still had this little girl that we, you know, tried to save, tried to hide, tried to feed.
Well, my father was able to-- had a little, probably, money.
And they were just buying food on the black market.
You did get a five.
